coredata.dc:title: Poly(vinyl alcohol)-based multifunctional smart packaging films with carbon dots-loaded metal-organic frameworks for freshness indicator and shelf-life extension of shrimp

coredata.dc:description: This study aims to fabricate poly(vinyl alcohol)-based films (PVA-based films) incorporated with carbon dots-loaded metal-organic frameworks (CD@MOF) for freshness indicator and shelf life extension of shrimp. Incorporation of CD@MOF to PVA-based composite films (PVA/CD@MOF) showed excellent antimicrobial and antioxidant activities with 77.44 % ABTS radical scavenging efficiency. Additionally, the composite films showed a superior UV-barrier of 99.48 % compared to 23.28 % for neat PVA film. The color of PVA/CD@MOF composite films changed from green to blue when exposed to ammonia. In shrimp freshness monitoring tests, the color of PVA/CD@MOF composite films turned brown simultaneously with changes in the chemical parameters of shrimp, providing a visual indication of changes in the quality of stored shrimp. Additionally, PVA/CD@MOF films effectively inhibited weight loss, pH increase, microbial growth, and oxidation of stored shrimp. These results imply that CD@MOF can serve effectively as a dual-purpose active and indicator additive in practical packaging materials.

head.ce:title: Poly(vinyl alcohol)-based multifunctional smart packaging films with carbon dots-loaded metal-organic frameworks for freshness indicator and shelf-life extension of shrimp

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to fabricate poly(vinyl alcohol)-based films (PVA-based films) incorporated with carbon dots-loaded metal-organic frameworks (CD@MOF) for freshness indicator and shelf life extension of shrimp. Incorporation of CD@MOF to PVA-based composite films (PVA/CD@MOF) showed excellent antimicrobial and antioxidant activities with 77.44 % ABTS radical scavenging efficiency. Additionally, the composite films showed a superior UV-barrier of 99.48 % compared to 23.28 % for neat PVA film. The color of PVA/CD@MOF composite films changed from green to blue when exposed to ammonia. In shrimp freshness monitoring tests, the color of PVA/CD@MOF composite films turned brown simultaneously with changes in the chemical parameters of shrimp, providing a visual indication of changes in the quality of stored shrimp. Additionally, PVA/CD@MOF films effectively inhibited weight loss, pH increase, microbial growth, and oxidation of stored shrimp. These results imply that CD@MOF can serve effectively as a dual-purpose active and indicator additive in practical packaging materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As consumers become increasingly concerned about their health, there is a growing demand for food that not only meets strict safety standards but also maintains freshness over the long term [1]. However, with the international expansion of food distribution networks and the increasing complexity of distribution networks, it is becoming increasingly difficult to maintain the quality and integrity of perishable items during their journey from farm to table [2]. To address these issues, packaging technologies designed to track and prolong the freshness of perishable products simultaneously are urgently needed. These advances have the potential to enhance food safety, reduce food waste, and ultimately improve consumer satisfaction.

Accordingly, the food packaging industry has developed smart packaging systems, for increasing overall food quality and safety. Smart packaging systems are essential for maintaining food quality across the supply chain, offering valuable insights through continuous monitoring [3]. As active packaging materials, carbon quantum dots [4], metal-based nanoparticles [5], essential oils [6], natural clay [7], and natural plant extracts [8] have been incorporated to the packaging films to preserve food quality. To monitor food quality, various types of natural pigments, including anthocyanins [9], carotenoids [10], and chlorophylls [11], have been used as indicators to detect changes in pH, ammonia levels, microbial metabolites, and substances associated with food spoilage [12].

Despite advancements in developing dual-function packaging solutions for preserving and monitoring food quality, most studies have focused only on individual (active or intelligent) functions. There is little research on packaging films with both active and intelligent functions. Additionally, research on dual-functional packaging materials is limited to the integration of active substances and natural pigments (anthocyanins) [13,14]. However, many natural pigments require improvement in certain aspects due to their potential interaction with food ingredients and their susceptibility to risk of deterioration from multiple environmental influences like oxidation, hydration, ultraviolet light exposure, temperature fluctuations, and enzyme activity [12]. Addressing these issues requires the development of robust freshness indicators with antibacterial and antioxidant properties. Nanotechnology is one of the promising techniques to achieve these goals.

Recently, the significance of nanotechnology in enhancing the durability, functionality, and physical characteristics of packaging materials has garnered considerable attention [15]. In particular, the development of nanoparticles, including carbon quantum dots (CD) and metal-organic frameworks (MOF) has opened up new possibilities for creating innovative packaging solutions. CD has garnered significant attention in biological research owing to its unique attributes, such as exceptional tissue compatibility, intrinsic photoluminescence, and minimal toxicity to living organisms [4]. Recent investigations have underscored the remarkable antioxidant, UV-blocking, and antibacterial properties of CD, making them promising candidates for active packaging materials. Moreover, the pH responsiveness and ammonia sensing ability of CD has been reported for their properties and application in food packaging [16]. The distinctive architecture of MOF, which comprises a crystalline lattice of metal-ion nodes linked by organic ligands, imparts numerous advantageous properties, such as a high surface area, substantial pore volume, and excellent structural stability [17–19]. MOF are capable of being precisely tailored to exhibit specific pore sizes and functionality under desired conditions, making them versatile candidates for a variety of applications in food packaging [20]. Metal ions such as Fe, Co, Cu, and Zn are frequently employed in the creation of MOF for various biological applications [21]. Notably, Cu-based MOF has received considerable attention because of the intrinsic antibacterial properties of Cu2+, making them adaptable substances for use in biomedical applications either independently or as components of composite incorporating MOF [22].

This research aimed to develop novel smart packaging films by incorporating CD@MOF into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix. The working hypothesis suggested that the combined properties of CD and MOF within the CD@MOF hybrid structure would enhance the overall functionality of the films, enabling quality monitoring while providing antimicrobial and antioxidative properties. In the CD@MOF hybrid system, CD demonstrates inherent abilities to reduce ultraviolet radiation, neutralize reactive oxygen species, and exhibit pH-responsive color changes. These combined traits enhance food preservation and enable consumer-oriented quality monitoring through visual indicators [4]. On the other hand, incorporating MOF promotes antibacterial effects and color changes in response to environmental changes, enabling sensitive detection of spoilage indicators such as pH change or ammonia production. The extensive porosity of MOF enables superior CD retention and controlled release, thereby enhancing its functional performance [23]. The combination of CD and MOF leverages their individual strengths to create materials with improved properties that surpass those of the individual components. However, research on utilizing CD@MOF in food packaging remains relatively scarce despite the enormous potential of these solutions to preserve and monitor food quality. Multifunctional packaging films based on PVA were developed by integrating CD@MOF at 2 wt% and 4 wt% concentrations. A comprehensive analysis was performed on their structural, morphological, chemical, and physical properties. Furthermore, the antibacterial and antioxidant properties were evaluated, and then their effectiveness for monitoring and preserving of shrimp quality was investigated. This study signifies a substantial advancement in smart food preservation technology, introducing a novel approach to enhance the stability and efficacy of CD and MOF in packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots-loaded metal-organic frameworks (CD@MOF)
ce:section.ce:para: CD was synthesized through a hydrothermal process involving citric acid and urea, as shown in Fig. 1 (Step 1). First, 3.75 g of citric acid was dissolved in 45 mL of water to form an aqueous solution. Then, 0.75 g of urea was precisely mixed with the mixture. This solution was subjected to hydrothermal treatment in a Teflon-lined pressure vessel at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the product was purified by filtering through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove residual contaminants, resulting in refined CD.

MOF was synthesized using a solvothermal process as described in our earlier study (Fig. 1 Step 2) [23]. CD@MOF was prepared by firstly suspending 0.4 g of CD in 20 mL of 99.9 % pure ethanol, and then adding 200 mg of MOF to the CD mixture (Fig. 1 Step 3). The composite solution was then subjected to vacuum-assisted dehydration at 50 °C, with vacuum activation for 30 min, followed by gradual air ventilation for 30 min. This procedure was meticulously performed in triplicate to maximize loading efficiency and ensure uniform distribution of CD within the MOF structure. Afterward, the resultant was subsequently centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitated CD@MOF complex was dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 12 h. The drying process removed residual solvent and ensured the integrity of CD@MOF. Finally, the Loading capacity of CD in CD@MOF was calculated following the Hong et al. [23].

Specific surface area and porosity of MOF and CD@MOF were performed via nitrogen (N2) gas adsorption at a temperature of 77 K (−196 °C) using a BELSORP-MAX instrument (MicrotracBEL Corp., Osaka, Japan). Prior to the adsorption test, a careful degassing process was conducted at 90 °C for 9 h to remove organic substances and ensure the reliability of the measurements. The specific surface area was subsequently determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of polyvinyl alcohol-based films embedded with CD@MOF (PVA/CD@MOF)
ce:section.ce:para: PVA (4 g) and glycerol (1.2 g) were dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water. The mixture was stirred at 95 °C for 1 h to ensure complete dissolution. Then, various amounts of CD@MOF (0, 0.08, and 0.16 g for 0, 2, and 4 wt% of PVA) were incorporated into the solution with continuous stirring. After adding CD@MOF, the solution was further stirred to achieve homogeneity. The prepared solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined glass plate and left to dry at 25 °C for 72 h. The fabricated films (PVA-based films) were labeled as neat PVA (0 % CD@MOF), PVA/CD@MOF2% (2 % CD@MOF), and PVA/CD@MOF4% (4 % CD@MOF).

To assess its effectiveness in preserving shrimp and monitoring spoilage during storage, PVA/CD@MOF2% composite film was employed. Prior to application, both the neat PVA and PVA/CD@MOF2% films were cut into 10 cm × 10 cm squares. The surfaces were then subjected to UV irradiation for 15 min for sterilization. A sterilized film was then placed over a sterile container (8 cm × 8 cm × 5 cm) containing two fresh shrimps (~40 g) and the lid tightly sealed over it. The characteristics of preservation and monitoring of shrimp freshness were examined at 72-h intervals throughout an 18-day refrigerated storage period maintained at 4 °C.

For the applicability of freshness preservation, various characteristics of shrimp were evaluated, such as weight loss, pH, color, bacterial growth, and oxidation value. The weight of shrimp was measured at the beginning of storage and on days 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, and 18. The weight loss (%) was evaluated by calculating the percentage decrease compared to the initial weight recorded at the beginning of storage. The pH and redness of shrimp were measured using a digital pH meter and a chroma meter, respectively. The quantification of the oxidation value and bacteria growth of packaged shrimp was performed according to the methods reported in previous study [24].
For its applicability in freshness monitoring, the visible color changes of PVA/CD@MOF2% film were evaluated through image capture, and the changes in surface color were analyzed.

Sample characteristics were measured in at least three replicates, with results presented as mean values and standard deviations. Statistical analysis of the experimental data was conducted using one-way ANOVA, followed by Duncan's multiple range tests, with significance defined at p < 0.05. These analyses were performed using SPSS software (version 26.0, SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CD, MOF, and CD@MOF
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a represents the particle size distribution of CD, with particle sizes ranging from 1.15 to 5.69 nm. The distribution shows that the majority of the carbon dots fall within this size range, with an average particle size of approximately 3.07 ± 1.19 nm. As shown in Fig. 2b, CD particles were small and exhibited a spherical shape. In contrast, MOF particles showed a polyhedron shape, with each particle having a porous structure and rough surface, as shown in Fig. 2c. The average particle size was measured to be 418.74 ± 90.44 nm. The rough surface of MOF contributes to the formation of its porous structure that can effectively encapsulate functional materials. The TEM image of CD@MOF confirmed the effective integration of CD into the pores of MOF, meeting expectations (Fig. 2d). Additionally, the structure of the CD@MOF particles remained polyhedron in shape, consistent with the MOF (Fig. 2c), indicating that loading CD did not change the structure of MOF. Furthermore, the particle size of CD@MOF was measured to be 377.64 ± 79.21 nm, similar to that of the MOF alone, further suggesting that the encapsulation of CD did not significantly alter the overall particle size of the composite material. This observation is in agreement with the previous report by Dang et al. [25], the acquired TEM images showed that the functional materials immobilized on the Cu-MOF surface did not change their native morphology.

To further verify the porosity of the MOF and the CD loading capacity on its surface and inside pores, N2 adsorption/desorption analysis was used in assessing the specific surface area, total pore volume, and CD loading capacity (Table S1, Supplementary). The examination of the synthesized MOF demonstrated remarkable porosity features, highlighted by a significant BET specific surface area of 1039.80 m2/g and a total pore volume of 0.63 cm3/g. These parameters highlight the highly porous nature of the synthesized material. Following this, CD was integrated into the MOF structure under vacuum conditions. This approach was chosen to ensure the effective infusion of CD molecules into the surface pores of the MOF matrix. Incorporating CD resulted in a reduction of the specific surface area to 454.70 m2/g and the total pore volume to 0.29 cm3/g, while achieving a loading efficiency of 39.61 %. These results suggest that CD can effectively bind and potentially block specific surface pores. The presence of functional materials within the porous MOF structure may limit nitrogen uptake [26]. Therefore, the observed decrease in total pore volume and BET surface area in CD@MOF suggests the presence of CD within the pores. This observation aligns with the previous report by Wiwasuku et al. [27] that CD-loaded MOF reduced pore volume and pore diameter compared to neat MOF.

The luminescence behavior of samples was performed by measuring the fluorescence emission under 370 nm excitation (Fig. 2e). The results showed that CD has strong emission characteristics, whereas MOF did not show emission properties. However, CD@MOF showed a fluorescent response, indicating that the CD was successfully loaded into the MOF. A prominent emission peak at 444 nm appeared in the CD fluorescence spectra. Conversely, the emission peak of CD@MOF shifted to 447 nm compared to that of CD, possibly due to the conjugation effect of H3BTC in MOF [28]. Additionally, the bright blue fluorescence of CD and CD@MOF aqueous solutions was clearly visible under UV (365 nm) light excitation (Fig. 2e).

The ability to absorb UV light is an important property of packaging materials, preventing deterioration and spoilage of food under sunlight. Fig. 2f illustrates the ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometric profiles of the synthesized particle. The CD spectroscopic analysis revealed two distinct peaks: a peak at 215 nm attributed to π-π⁎ electronic transitions of surface-bound CC structures, and a peak at 330 nm indicative of n-π⁎ transitions within carbonyl groups and CN linkages [29]. The synthesized metal-organic framework exhibited a distinct spectral peak in the 210–240 nm range, attributable to π-π⁎ transitions within the H3BTC molecular building blocks [30]. CD@MOF exhibited absorption between 210 and 230 nm and 330 nm, which is consistent with the unique absorption bands of CD and MOF. These observations imply that CD was successfully incorporated into the MOF structure.

The functional groups present in samples were examined with FT-IR spectroscopy. As presented in Fig. 2g, several peaks were observed within the range of 4000–550 cm−1. The broad range of peaks from 3500 to 2500 cm−1 in the CD spectrum corresponded to the stretching vibrations of NH and OH bonds [31]. The CO bond was evident in the region around 1650 cm−1. The peak detected at 1535 cm−1 is associated with the CC stretching vibration, which signifies the polymerization of urea and citric acid, thereby confirming the successful synthesis of CD [32]. Moreover, a characteristic CH stretching vibration appeared at 1455 cm−1. The vibrational spectrum displayed prominent absorption signals at 1377 cm−1, 1319 cm−1, and 1187 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, and CN bonds, respectively [33]. These functional groups were also characteristic of citric acid or urea, providing evidence for the successful synthesis of CD from urea and citric acid [32]. Absorption bands were observed at approximately 1045 cm−1 for CH2-NH2 and approximately 670 cm−1 for urea-derived secondary amines [34]. The FTIR spectrum of the MOF showed spectral features at 1644 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1, which were linked to the vibrational modes of CO stretching and C − O asymmetric stretching, respectively [23]. Additionally, the band observed at 1370 cm−1 corresponded to the bending of the O − H functional groups [35]. Consistent with the results reported by Kanno et al. [36], the spectral feature at 1240 cm−1 was identified as representing C–OH vibrations linked to carboxylic acid groups. Multiple broad bands in the range 1000–1100 cm−1 corresponded to the Cu–OH vibrations [37]. The strong band observed at 760 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of MOF particles was associated with the CH vibration absorption of benzene [38]. Additionally, the spectral feature at 727 cm−1 was linked to the vibrational modes of the crystal lattice, particularly those involving CuO, Cu–O–Cu, and O–Cu–O bonding connections [22]. The spectrum of CD@MOF revealed distinctive peaks representing both CD and MOF, confirming the successful loading of CD into the MOF structure. Additionally, the slight shift and increase in the intensity of the peak associated with CD@MOF compared to MOF may result from the overlap of similar functional groups within their respective IR spectra of the MOF and CD [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional properties of CD, MOF, and CD@MOF
ce:section.ce:para: Lipid oxidation along with bacterial growth is major cause of spoilage in a variety of foods [40]. Oxidation of various food components can produce free radicals, which may degrade the quality and integrity of food products. The use of appropriate antioxidants has been proven to be effective in neutralizing free radicals and delaying oxidation [41]. The antioxidant efficiency of samples was evaluated with ABTS (2,2-Azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) and DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) radical scavenging assays (Fig. 3a). As a result, the ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging efficiency of CD increased from 23.84 ± 0.82 % to 80.72 ± 1.85 % and 21.95 ± 1.07 % to 75.58 ± 0.54 % across an ascending concentration gradient from 100 to 500 μg/mL, respectively. CD, containing carboxyl and amino groups, has been reported to have excellent free radical scavenging ability [42]. The sample's antioxidant capacity is linked to its molecular structure, which contains functional groups such as carboxylic acids (−COOH), hydroxyls (−OH), and amines (−NH2,−NH). These groups enable hydrogen atom transfer, converting reactive oxygen species (ROS∙) into their reduced forms (ROS-H). Additionally, the CD surface has unpaired electrons that can undergo electron delocalization. This process happens either within the aromatic regions of the molecule or through intramolecular bond reorganization [43]. The MOF also exhibited antioxidant activity, but its ABTS radical scavenging efficiency was only 4.45 ± 0.59 % at 100 μg/mL. As the MOF concentration increased from 100 to 500 μg/mL, the efficiency improved and reached 37.10 ± 0.81 %. However, MOF showed no measurable DPPH radical scavenging activity even at concentrations as high as 300 μg/mL, and a slight activity of 1.93 ± 1.76 % was observed at 400 μg/mL, and slightly increased to 18.39 ± 1.67 % at 500 μg/mL. The capacity for neutralizing free radicals through antioxidant activity of MOF arises from edge site features containing −NH2, −OH, and − COOH groups, known as H-donating properties [16]. Substantial evidence supports the hypothesis that the MOF's peroxidase-mimicking properties underlie its radical scavenging mechanism [44]. After CD loading, the reduction of oxidative reactions mediated by ABTS and DPPH were further improved from 37.10 ± 0.80 % to 55.53 ± 1.17 % and 18.39 ± 1.67 % to 31.99 ± 0.71 %, respectively, at 500 μg/mL. Various studies have highlighted the radical scavenging potential of CD [45,46] and MOF [9,15], indicating their suitability as functional additives in the active packaging industry. However, there are currently no reports of the improved antioxidant activity by loading CD into MOF, and the improved antioxidant activity observed for CD@MOF suggests its potential as a functional component in advanced food packaging solutions.

Bacterial growth poses a serious threat to the overall freshness of food and is able to accelerate undesirable alterations in taste, color, and texture along with odor development [47]. Integration of microbicidal compounds within packaging films is essential for minimizing food spoilage and contamination during various stages such as handling, transportation, and storage, and to act as a protective barrier to minimize bacterial risks and maintain food quality. The antibacterial efficiency of samples was evaluated utilizing the agar well diffusion assay, with results depicted in Fig. 3b–e and Table S2 (Supplementary). For all bacterial species tested, the CD sample did not produce any observable zones of inhibition. The inhibition zone sizes of MOF were 26.03 ± 0.82 mm, 27.76 ± 0.10 mm, 14.94 ± 0.07 mm, and 26.85 ± 0.26 mm for Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella enterica, Escherichia coli, and Listeria monocytogenes, respectively. The inhibition zone did not significantly (p > 0.05) increase after CD loading for all tested bacteria compared to MOF. A quantitative evaluation of the antimicrobial efficacy of CD, MOF, and CD@MOF was performed using a 96-well microdilution method (Table S2, Supplementary). The MIC/MBC values of CD against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Listeria monocytogenes were > 1000/>1000 μg/mL, whereas for Salmonella enterica were 800/900 μg/mL. The MIC/MBC values of both MOF and CD@MOF were consistent at 25/50 μg/mL for Salmonella enterica, 500/600 μg/mL for Escherichia coli, and 100/200 μg/mL for Staphylococcus aureus and Listeria monocytogenes. The dissociation of Cu2+ ions from the MOF structure is proposed as the primary mechanism underlying its bactericidal properties, as these ions disrupt essential cellular activities [48]. The safety of copper in MOF has been extensively studied, with studies showing negligible toxicity even at relatively high concentrations [49,50], making it suitable for various biomedical applications including active packaging. Additionally, the bactericidal activity of MOF is attributed to their crystalline structure, altered ion concentration balance, and metal-ligand coordination system, which overall improves its antibacterial properties [51]. The MOF's extensive internal surface area, defined by its intricate structure, offers numerous sites for bacterial adhesion, increasing exposure to metal ions and leading to a stronger bactericidal effect [9]. Although loading CD does not significantly improve the antibacterial activity of MOF, the CD@MOF exhibits strong antibacterial activity that is inherently derived from MOF. The resulting CD@MOF eliminates pathogenic and spoilage-causing microorganisms and shows potential applications as an active agent in multifunctional films.

Fig. 4a shows the apparent appearance of PVA-based films and Table S3 (Supplementary) provides the surface color parameters of these films. Neat PVA film was colorless and showed distinct high transparency with an L-value (lightness) of 90.70 ± 0.52. However, incorporating CD@MOF to the PVA substrate caused a significant reduction in film brightness. The composite films exhibited statistically significant (p < 0.05) decreases in luminosity, with measured values of 76.94 ± 0.39 and 64.33 ± 0.89 for 2 % and 4 % CD@MOF loadings, respectively, compared to the neat PVA. Upon the addition of CD@MOF, PVA/CD@MOF exhibited a green tint whose intensity varied with concentration, and this phenomenon was ascribed to the intrinsic greenish hue of the CD@MOF. Consequently, greenness was decreased from 4.38 ± 0.03 for neat PVA film to −8.01 ± 0.32 for PVA/CD@MOF4% film and yellowness was increased from −0.26 ± 0.06 for neat PVA film to 14.79 ± 0.08 for PVA/CD@MOF4% film. Using the ΔE value of the neat PVA film as the reference, the integration of CD@MOF into the PVA-based matrices resulted in a significant enhancement of the ΔE parameter. The ΔE for the PVA/CD@MOF2% and PVA/CD@MOF4% film increased to 17.54 ± 1.27 and 31.31 ± 0.13, respectively. These results demonstrate a substantial change in the color attributes of the films with the incorporation of CD@MOF, and the magnitude of the ΔE increase is more pronounced as the concentration of CD@MOF increases.

Exposure to light can deteriorate food quality, decompose nutrients and bioactive compounds, accelerate oxidation, and produce harmful substances. However, the visible light transmittance through the film is an important factor in its suitability for various packaging applications [52]. Table S3 (Supplementary) presents quantitative data on the optical transparency and UV-blocking performance of the PVA-based. Spectrophotometric analysis of the neat PVA film showed significant optical permeability in both the UV and visible spectra, with transmittance values of 77.45 ± 0.64 % at 280 nm and 90.75 ± 0.49 % at 660 nm, respectively. The inclusion of CD@MOF into the PVA matrix resulted in a statistically significant (p < 0.05) improvement in UV-blocking capabilities. Notably, a 4 % CD@MOF loading reduced the T280 value to 0.52 ± 0.11 %. Additionally, the addition of 4 % CD@MOF significantly (p < 0.05) reduced the transparency from 90.75 ± 0.49 % to 27.89 ± 2.51 %. The improvement in UV-barrier ability may be due to the abundance of aromatic systems, which enhance electronic transitions in the ultraviolet spectral region [53]. Moreover, the observed UV barrier properties in the composite films may originate from the UV-absorbing ability of CD. Therefore, the incorporation of CD@MOF gave the film significant (p < 0.05) UV absorption ability owing to the synergetic UV absorption characteristics of CD and MOF. These properties make the composite films particularly suitable for packaging foods sensitive to UV radiation, especially those with elevated lipid content [54].

Fig. 4b shows SEM micrographs illustrating the fabricated film's surface characteristics. Neat PVA exhibited a uniform surface with a few small clumps. Both 2 wt% and 4 wt% CD@MOF showed uniform distribution within the PVA polymer framework, suggesting robust interfacial compatibility and the formation of a unified, densely interconnected composite structure [9,55]. Cross-sectional analysis of neat PVA film revealed a homogeneous morphology with no signs of cracking or aggregation, suggesting well-integrated polymer chains. In contrast, the cross-sections of the PVA/CD@MOF composite films revealed a denser and more uniform structure without evidence of phase separation, suggesting strong compatibility and beneficial interactions within the filler-polymer blend system [15]. Consequently, the potential H-bond interactions between the functional groups of CD@MOF and PVA may result in more uniform and densely packed structure.

The chemical composition of the PVA-based films was analyzed using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 5a). The FT-IR spectra of the neat PVA film showed characteristic absorption bands, including OH stretching vibrations at 3275 cm−1 and CH stretching modes at 2950 cm−1, 2917 cm−1, and 1313 cm−1, consistent with previous studies [56,57]. The distinct absorption peak centered at 1631 cm−1 is indicative of CO bond elongation [58]. Moreover, a distinct peak at 1529 cm−1, attributed to the vibrational signature of carbonyl functionalities, was prominently observed [59]. The results align with the hypothesis that PVA experienced oxidative chain scission, driven by radicals produced during the heat-induced dissolution process in an open-air environment [60]. Spectroscopic analysis revealed a distinctive absorption band at 1070 cm−1, attributed to C–OH bond stretching vibrations [57]. Additionally, for CO symmetric stretching, absorption bands appeared at 906 cm−1 and 818 cm−1 [58]. Comparative analysis of the FTIR spectra from PVA/CD@MOF composite films and the meat PVA showed largely similar spectral features. Owing to the resemblance in surface functional group arrangement between PVA and CD@MOF, the characteristic bands may overlap or superimpose within the composite films. The peaks corresponding to OH stretching (3275 cm−1–3247 cm−1) and CH stretching and bending (from 2917 cm−1 to 2899 cm−1) observed in neat PVA film were shifted in the PVA/CD@MOF films [46]. These changes were attributed to the creation of hydrogen-bonding networks, both intra- and intermolecular, involving the CD@MOF structure and the PVA polymer matrix [23,58]. Furthermore, with CD@MOF incorporation, additional characteristic peaks existed at 1604 cm−1 and 1378 cm−1 of PVA/CD@MOF compared with neat PVA film, confirming the successful integration of CD@MOF to PVA matrix.

The durability and strength of packaging films are vital for their ability to endure the stresses of storage and transportation processes. The mechanical properties including tensile strength (TS), Young's modulus (YM), and elongation at break (EB) of the PVA-based films are summarized in Table 1 . The thickness of the PVA-based films increased noticeably from 55.86 ± 3.14 μm for neat PVA film to 77.54 ± 7.94 μm for PVA/CD@MOF2% film and 77.29 ± 4.70 μm for PVA/CD@MOF4% film, which is attributed to the increasing in solid particle content. Neat PVA film showed TS of 35.42 ± 1.86 MPa, YM of 225.45 ± 29.63 MPa, and EB of 273.70 ± 43.80 %, and CD@MOF incorporation improved the strength (TS) and flexibility (EB) but decreased stiffness (YM). The TS and EB of PVA/CD@MOF2% increased to 37.48 ± 1.68 MPa and 324.64 ± 15.53 %, respectively, but its YM decreased to 169.82 ± 25.83 MPa. In PVA/CD@MOF4% film, both TS and EB further increased to 41.95 ± 2.50 MPa and 381.65 ± 20.42 MPa, respectively, while YM had no significant effect compared to PVA/CD@MOF2% film. The improved mechanical properties observed for PVA/CD@MOF films are hypothesized to result from the formation of interfacial hydrogen bonding networks at the MOF-polymer interface [61], which is consistent with the FT-IR data (Fig. 5a). Dai et al. [62] also reported that the addition of MOF to PVA improved the strength of the polymer backbone. Additionally, the presence of CD has been reported to act as a lubricant within the film, increasing the elongation and reducing the stiffness of the films [63]. As a result, the combined effect of CD and MOF improved both the strength and flexibility of the PVA polymer matrix, revealing their suitability for packaging systems.

Table 1 summarizes the WVP and WCA results of PVA-based films. The WVP of a film is important to preserve the safety and quality of stored food. Film may possess different levels of WVP depending on specific application requirements [64]. Neat PVA film exhibited a WVP of 8.11 ± 0.23 × 10−9 g·m/m2·Pa·s. With the addition of CD@MOF, the WVP of the PVA-based films decreased to 7.26 ± 0.15 × 10−9 g·m/m2·Pa·s and 6.76 ± 0.22 × 10−9 g·m/m2·Pa·s with the addition of 2 wt% and 4 wt% CD@MOF, respectively. This phenomenon can be attributed to the well-dispersed CD@MOF particles and their excellent interfacial adhesion with the PVA matrix. As a result, this increased the tortuous path of molecular permeation, which reduces the WVP of PVA/CD@MOF films [15]. Similar results were also reported by Ma et al. [65] and Chen et al. [66].

Neat PVA film showed a WCA of 49.18 ± 3.88°, indicating its hydrophilic nature. The addition of CD@MOF to the PVA results in a noticeable increase in WCA depending on the CD@MOF content. The WCA of PVA/CD@MOF films significantly (p < 0.05) increased from 49.18 ± 3.88° for neat PVA film to 56.97 ± 2.93° and 67.10 ± 2.58° for 2 wt% and 4 wt% CD@MOF, respectively. The observed increase can be attributed to the relatively higher hydrophobicity of CD@MOF relative to the PVA matrix. The increase in WCA observed in PVA/CD@MOF films is believed to result from a reduction in overall film hydrophilicity, a trend that aligns with the WVP measurements obtained. Similarly, incorporating Cu-MOF into the cellulose acetate matrix also increased WCA [67].

Packaging films enriched with suitable antioxidants are crucial for extending the shelf life of packaged products by neutralizing or removing harmful agents, such as radical oxidative species produced during food storage [68]. Fig. 5b shows the antioxidant activity of PVA-based films, assessed by their ABTS radical scavenging capacity. The antioxidant efficacy of neat PVA film was 10.94 ± 1.07 %. However, PVA/CD@MOF2% film and PVA/CD@MOF4% film showed ABTS radical scavenging activities of 40.47 ± 2.30 % and 77.44 ± 0.50 %, respectively. The improved free radical neutralization efficacy of CD@MOF is attributed to the synergistic scavenging capabilities of both CD and MOF. ABTS radical scavenging activity is ascribed to the hydrogen-donating ability of the hydroxyl and amide groups present in CD [69]. Conversely, MOF exhibits free radical neutralization capabilities through the transfer of hydrogen atoms or electrons from its peripheral functional groups, including amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl moieties. This action neutralizes free radicals, quenches singlet and triplet oxygens, and the degradation of superoxide entities [23]. The synergistic effect between CD and MOF contributes to the antioxidant performance of CD@MOF particles exhibiting a concentration-dependent response. The antioxidant efficacy of CD@MOF is enhanced at higher concentrations, which may be due to the increased availability of active sites for neutralizing radicals. These results validate the excellent antioxidant properties of PVA/CD@MOF films, indicating their suitability for active packaging application of lipid-rich foods.

The inclusion of antimicrobial properties in food packaging materials is crucial for improving food preservation and extending product shelf life by inhibiting the growth of spoilage microorganisms and pathogenic bacteria [70]. To evaluate these properties, the antibacterial properties of PVA/CD@MOF films were tested using a time-kill assay (Fig. 6 ). Neat PVA film did not show any antibacterial properties, indicating the lack of significant antibacterial activity of the polysaccharide components [71]. In contrast, PVA/CD@MOF films showed notable antibacterial efficacy against Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella enterica, Escherichia coli, and Listeria monocytogenes. Specifically, PVA/CD@MOF2% film showed a significant decline in the colony count of Staphylococcus aureus and Listeria monocytogenes by around 3.4 Log CFU/mL and 3.1 Log CFU/mL, respectively, within 3 h after exposure. Additionally, PVA/CD@MOF2% film reduced the colony numbers of Salmonella enterica and Escherichia coli by around 2.5 Log CFU/mL and 1.4 Log CFU/mL, respectively, within 3 h after exposure. PVA/CD@MOF2% film was shown to completely inhibit bacterial growth in all strains within 6 h after exposure. However, fPVA/CD@MOF4% film showed complete growth inhibition for all bacteria tested within just 3 h after exposure. Empirical data indicate that CD@MOF exerts a more significant inhibitory effect on the growth of Gram-positive pathogens, surpassing its efficacy against Gram-negative pathogens. This appears to be due to an inherent structural imbalance. The single peptidoglycan layer of Gram-positive bacterial cell walls, with outward-facing peptide chains, enhances the affinity for CD and MOF interactions, facilitating their penetration into the cytoplasm [72]. Conversely, Gram-negative bacteria are characterized by a thin peptidoglycan layer surrounded by an outer membrane composed of lipopolysaccharides, which complicates the interactions between CD and MOF. These results suggest that they are consistent with previous research results of various CD types [46,73] and MOF types [74,75].

Color-responsive smart films are employed for real-time quality evaluation of proteinaceous foodstuffs, including shrimp and meat, by reacting with volatile chemicals like ammonia that are released during the spoilage process. Ammonia-dependent color change of PVA-based films is depicted in Fig. 7 . Neat PVA did not show any color transition with exposure to ammonia vapor because polysaccharides lack functional groups that react with ammonia vapor. A slight darkening of color can be seen with the naked eye in PVA/CD2% and PVA/CD4% films. Several studies have shown that CD-based yellow color films/papers shifted to darker brown shades in response to NH3 vapor [76,77]. Meanwhile, PVA/MOF2% and PVA/MOF4% films showed average and excellent color changes, respectively. PVA/CD@MOF2% and PVA/CD@MOF4% films exhibited excellent and distinct color changes from green to blue during exposure to ammonia vapor, demonstrating the synergistic effect of CD and MOF on ammonia-induced color Transition. Quantitative color parameters were further analyzed during exposure to ammonia (Fig. S1, supplementary). Exposure of the PVA/CD film to ammonia changed the yellow tint of the film to a darker color (evidenced by a rise in the b-value), indicating the ammonia-sensitive nature of the CD. Additionally, a significant increase in a-value, indicating a decrease in green color, and a sharp decrease in b-value, indicating an increase in blue color, were observed for both PVA/MOF film and PVA/CD@MOF film (Fig. S1b and c, Supplementary). These findings confirm that the film color transitions from green to blue. Interestingly, the color difference results shown in Fig. S1d (Supplementary) show that the color change induced by 2 % CD@MOF is very similar to the effect observed by adding 4 % MOF. This means that the synergistic effect of CD and MOF leads to improved ammonia detection in the composite. Exposure to ammonia gas alkalizes the CD solution and increases its pH. This pH-induced aggregation subsequently deepens the color of the CD and quenches its fluorescence [78]. The interaction between NH3 and copper can result in significant electron transfer (NH3 core to copper), potentially contributing to notable changes in the CD@MOF's original electron transfer mechanism [79]. The reaction between copper-based MOF and ammonia leads to electron transfer, which reduces the copper oxide to copper ions and potentially changes the color to blue [80]. Therefore, the combined ammonia sensing effect of each CD and MOF induced a favorable color change in the proposed PVA/CD@MOF film, suggesting its potential as an active indicator film for freshness tracking and preservation of food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PVA-based films for shrimp packaging

ce:section.ce:para: The high susceptibility of shrimp to spoilage and limited shelf life are current challenges in maintaining shrimp integrity throughout storage and distribution. The PVA/CD@MOF2% film was selected for the storage test as this concentration showed sufficient antimicrobial activity. The usefulness of PVA/CD@MOF2% film was investigated through the packaging of fresh shrimp refrigerated at 4 °C over an 18-day period and the chemical, physical, and microbial properties were monitored. The weight loss of the samples was found to increase noticeably over time (Fig. 8a). For neat PVA film, the growth rate accelerated noticeably after 9 days. Conversely, shrimp packaged with PVA/CD@MOF2% film showed a significantly slower growth rate compared to those packaged with neat PVA film. This means that PVA/CD@MOF2% film provides a promising option for mitigating moisture loss in shrimp. Additionally, the pH value of shrimp packaged with PVA/CD@MOF2% film gradually increased from 6.86 to 7.49 during storage, while the pH of shrimp packaged with neat PVA film increased from 6.86 to 7.96 (Fig. 8b). As the pH of shrimp increases, their quality decreases and shrimp with a pH 7.3 is unsatisfactory for consumers [81]. Shrimp packaged with neat PVA film exceeded pH 7.3 between 3 and 6 days after storage, whereas shrimp packaged with PVA/CD@MOF2% film maintained pH level within acceptable limits for up to 12 days. As the quality of the shrimp decreased (up to pH 7.3), the apparent color of the shrimp increased to red (above 4) at 6 days in neat PVA film and 12 days in PVA/CD@MOF2% film (Fig. 8c).

The effectiveness of PVA/CD@MOF2% film in inhibiting oxidation was assessed through the TBARS test, used to quantify the level of lipid oxidation byproducts as an indicator of oxidative damage (Fig. 8d). The TBARS assay is among the earliest and most extensively employed methods for detecting malondialdehyde (MDA), a reactive aldehyde generated through the lipid peroxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids [24]. Initial levels of TBARS in shrimp were recorded to be approximately 0.20 mg MDA/kg. However, for shrimp stored in neat PVA film and PVA/CD@MOF2%, these levels increased significantly to approximately 0.97 mg MDA/kg and 0.53 mg MDA/kg, respectively, after an 18-day storage period. These results indicate that PVA/CD@MOF2% film is effective in protecting shrimp from lipid oxidation.

The efficacy of PVA/CD@MOF2% film on bacterial growth was evaluated using TVC, a well-known method for assessing microbial contamination in food (Fig. 8e). According to previous studies [82,83], shrimp samples were considered spoiled when TVC exceeded 6.0 log CFU/g. Following an 18 days period, shrimp packaged with PVA/CD@MOF2% film showed significantly lower TVC compared to packaged with neat PVA film. The TVC of shrimp in the neat PVA film rose from 0 Log CFU/mL to 8.86 Log CFU/mL, while for the PVA/CD@MOF2% film, the TVC increased from 0 Log CFU/mL to 6.67 Log CFU/mL. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of PVA/CD@MOF2% film through the synergistic interactions between the polymer matrix, CD, and MOF. The PVA matrix acts as a flexible support for the uniform distribution of CD and MOF, allowing these components to interact efficiently with the food surface. The porous MOF structure provides a large surface area for adsorption of spoilage-related compounds such as ammonia, thereby delaying microbial growth and spoilage. In addition, MOF particles exhibit antibacterial activity, preventing the invasion of spoilage bacteria. At the same time, CDs possess strong antioxidant activity and UV protection property, which can delay the spoilage of food. Combining CD and MOF with the polymer matrix enhances both the antimicrobial activity and oxidation protection, making PVA/CD@MOF2% film a very effective material for extending the shelf life of food.

To assess the effectiveness of the color-responsive films, extended storage trials were conducted using shrimp. The apparent changes of PVA-based films (color indicator) stored at 4 °C for 18 days are shown in Fig. 9a. Fig. 9b and Fig. 9c depict the variations in the a and b values of the PVA-based films, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9a, PVA/CD@MOF2% film changes from initial green to brown with increasing storage time. As shown in Fig. 9b and Fig. 9c, neat PVA film shows little change in the a and b values during shrimp spoilage. In contrast, the PVA/CD@MOF2% exhibits a distinct color transition from initial green to brown after storage.

The observed color transition in fabricated films during shrimp storage was further confirmed with the pH dependent behavior of fabricated films (Fig. S2, Supplementary). The color change observed in the PVA/CD@MOF2% film is due to the pH-sensitivity of CD and MOF. Carbon dots change electronic structure with pH changes through protonation or deprotonation of surface functional groups, resulting in a noticeable color change. Similarly, MOF structure is sensitive to pH changes, which alter the metal-ligand coordination environment and further affect the optical properties. This synergistic effect between CD and MOF enhances the colorimetric response, which changes distinctly from green to brown as shrimp spoilage progresses. This pH-responsive behavior provides a reliable visual indicator for real-time freshness monitoring. PVA/CD@MOF films exhibited a color transition from green to brown approximately at pH 7. Therefore, this is consistent with the rapid color change of PVA/CD@MOF films to brown at day 12, when the pH level of shrimp reached approximately 7, indicating shrimp spoilage. These observations validate the potential applicability of the indicator as a visual measure of freshness. Here, green indicates freshness and brown indicates spoilage.

The antimicrobial effectiveness of MOF-loaded films has been extensively documented in previous studies, such as the application of Co-MOF in polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan nanofiber-based films, which demonstrated both ammonia detection and antimicrobial activity [84]. Similarly, the PVA/CD@MOF2% film in our study exhibited visually detectable color changes in response to ammonia (Figs. 7 and S1) and pH (Fig. S2), thereby showcasing its potential as an intelligent packaging material. This colorimetric behavior is comparable to that observed in other pH-sensitive packaging systems incorporating MOFs, which have been successfully used to monitor food freshness through visible color transitions [85].

Notably, previous studies primarily relied on the incorporation of external dye components into MOF structures to achieve such colorimetric responses [9]. In contrast, our approach utilizes the intrinsic color-changing properties of the MOF itself, which are significantly enhanced by the addition of carbon dots (CD). The synergistic effect between the MOF and CD results in more distinct and pronounced color changes, making the system more effective for real-time environmental monitoring without the need for additional dye components.

The developed PVA-based multifunctional smart packaging films incorporating CD@MOF showed promising potential to enhance and track the freshness of shrimp. This innovative composite films exhibited strong antibacterial and antioxidant activities along with excellent UV-barrier properties. Additionally, it effectively inhibited weight loss, pH increase, microbial growth, and oxidation of stored shrimp. The color change of PVA/CD@MOF film upon ammonia exposure provided a reliable visual indicator of changes in shrimp quality over time. Overall, the integration of CD into MOF (CD@MOF) stands out as a promising approach, suggesting a synergistic effect between CD and MOF that offers multifaceted functionality for smart packaging applications.

Su Jung Hong: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zohreh Riahi: Writing – review & editing. Gye Hwa Shin: Writing – review & editing. Jun Tae Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
